question: 7. Show that if the series \( a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+\cdots+a_{n}+\cdots \) converges, then the series \( a_{1}+a_{2} 2+a_{3}^{\prime} 3+\cdots+a_{n}^{\prime} n+\cdots \) converges also.
solution:
Solution. This is a special case of a result sometimes called Abel's summation theorem: If the partial sums of the series \( \Sigma a_{n} \) are bounded and the sequence \( \left\{b_{n}\right\} \) decreases to zero, then \( \Sigma a_{n} b_{n} \) is convergent.

This theorem can be proved as follows. Let \( s_{k}=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} \) and let \( M \) be a bound for \( \left\{\left|s_{k}\right|\right\} \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
a_{1} b_{1}+a_{2} b_{2} & +\cdots+a_{n} b_{n} \\
& =s_{1} b_{1}+\left(s_{2}-s_{1}\right) b_{2}+\cdots+\left(s_{n}-s_{n-1}\right) b_{n} \\
& =s_{1}\left(b_{1}-b_{2}\right)+s_{2}\left(b_{2}-b_{3}\right)+\cdots+s_{n-1}\left(b_{n-1}-b_{n}\right)+s_{n} b_{n}
\end{aligned}
\]
that is,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} b_{i}=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} s_{i}\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right)+s_{n} b_{n}
\]

Now the series \( \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} s_{i}\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right) \) is absolutely convergent, for
\[
\left|s_{i}\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right)\right| \leq M\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right)
\]
and \( \sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right) \) converges to \( b_{1} \).
Moreover, \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} s_{n} b_{n}=0 \) because \( \left\{s_{n}\right\} \) is bounded. Therefore,
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} b_{i}=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} s_{i}\left(b_{i}-b_{i+1}\right)
\]

Thus the theorem is proved.
For the particular problem, take \( b_{n}=1 / n \) and the result is immediate.

descriptive_long.question: 7. Show that if the series \( termone+termtwo+termthree+\cdots+termindex+\cdots \) converges, then the series \( termone+termtwo 2+termthree^{\prime} 3+\cdots+termindex^{\prime} indexvar+\cdots \) converges also.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. This is a special case of a result sometimes called Abel's summation theorem: If the partial sums of the series \( \Sigma termindex \) are bounded and the sequence \( \{coefindex\} \) decreases to zero, then \( \Sigma termindex\,coefindex \) is convergent.

This theorem can be proved as follows. Let \( partialk=\sum_{counteri=1}^{counterk} termgeneric \) and let \( boundmax \) be a bound for \( \{|partialk|\} \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
termone\,coefone+termtwo\,coeftwo & +\cdots+termindex\,coefindex \\
& =partialone\,coefone+\left(partialtwo-partialone\right)\,coeftwo+\cdots+\left(partialn-partialnprev\right)\,coefindex \\
& =partialone\left(coefone-coeftwo\right)+partialtwo\left(coeftwo-coefthree\right)+\cdots+partialnprev\left(b_{n-1}-coefindex\right)+partialn\,coefindex
\end{aligned}
\]
that is,
\[
\sum_{counteri=1}^{indexvar} termgeneric\,coefgeneric=\sum_{counteri=1}^{indexvar-1} partiali\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right)+partialn\,coefindex
\]

Now the series \( \sum_{counteri=1}^{\infty} partiali\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right) \) is absolutely convergent, for
\[
\left|partiali\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right)\right| \le boundmax\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right)
\]
and \( \sum_{counteri=1}^{\infty}\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right) \) converges to \( coefone \).
Moreover, \( \lim_{indexvar \to \infty} partialn\,coefindex=0 \) because \( \{partialn\} \) is bounded. Therefore,
\[
\lim_{indexvar \to \infty} \sum_{counteri=1}^{indexvar} termgeneric\,coefgeneric=\sum_{counteri=1}^{\infty} partiali\left(coefgeneric-coefnext\right)
\]

Thus the theorem is proved.
For the particular problem, take \( coefindex = 1 / indexvar \) and the result is immediate.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 7. Show that if the series \( butternut+candlewax+dragonfly+\cdots+elephanty+\cdots \) converges, then the series \( butternut+candlewax 2+a_{3}^{\prime} 3+\cdots+a_{n}^{\prime} tangerine+\cdots \) converges also.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. This is a special case of a result sometimes called Abel's summation theorem: If the partial sums of the series \( \Sigma elephanty \) are bounded and the sequence \( \left\{giraffery\right\} \) decreases to zero, then \( \Sigma elephanty giraffery \) is convergent.

This theorem can be proved as follows. Let \( nectarine=\sum_{violinist=1}^{umbrella} fireplace \) and let \( watermelon \) be a bound for \( \left\{\left|nectarine\right|\right\} \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
butternut\ jellybean+candlewax\ kaleidoso & +\cdots+elephanty\ giraffery \\
& =raspberry\ jellybean+\left(strawberry-raspberry\right)\ kaleidoso+\cdots+\left(quartzite-pomegranate\right)\ giraffery \\
& =raspberry\left(jellybean-kaleidoso\right)+strawberry\left(kaleidoso-lemongrass\right)+\cdots+pomegranate\left(b_{n-1}-giraffery\right)+quartzite\ giraffery
\end{aligned}
\]
that is,
\[
\sum_{violinist=1}^{tangerine} fireplace\ hazelnuts=\sum_{violinist=1}^{tangerine-1} orangutan\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right)+quartzite\ giraffery
\]

Now the series \( \sum_{violinist=1}^{\infty} orangutan\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right) \) is absolutely convergent, for
\[
\left|orangutan\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right)\right| \leq watermelon\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right)
\]
and \( \sum_{violinist=1}^{\infty}\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right) \) converges to \( jellybean \).
Moreover, \( \lim _{tangerine \rightarrow \infty} quartzite\ giraffery=0 \) because \( \left\{quartzite\right\} \) is bounded. Therefore,
\[
\lim _{tangerine \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{violinist=1}^{tangerine} fireplace\ hazelnuts=\sum_{violinist=1}^{\infty} orangutan\left(hazelnuts-marigolds\right)
\]

Thus the theorem is proved.
For the particular problem, take \( giraffery=1 / tangerine \) and the result is immediate.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 7. Show that if the series \( lastcomponent + penultimatepiece + finalfragment + \cdots + constantvalue + \cdots \) converges, then the series \( lastcomponent + penultimatepiece 2 + finalfragment^{\prime} 3 + \cdots + constantvalue^{\prime} beginner + \cdots \) converges also.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. This is a special case of a result sometimes called Abel's summation theorem: If the partial sums of the series \( \Sigma constantvalue \) are bounded and the sequence \( \{steadyfactor\} \) decreases to zero, then \( \Sigma constantvalue\, steadyfactor \) is convergent.

This theorem can be proved as follows. Let \( completesum = \sum_{aggregate=1}^{endpoint} unindexedwhole \) and let \( unbounded \) be a bound for \( \{\lvert completesum \rvert\} \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
lastcomponent\, terminalmass + penultimatepiece\, penultimatemass & + \cdots + constantvalue\, steadyfactor \\ & = remainder\, terminalmass + ( headlesssum - remainder )\, penultimatemass + \cdots + ( entiresum - nearlycompletesum )\, steadyfactor \\ & = remainder( terminalmass - penultimatemass ) + headlesssum( penultimatemass - antepenultimatemass ) + \cdots + nearlycompletesum( b_{beginner-1} - steadyfactor ) + entiresum\, steadyfactor
\end{aligned}
\]
that is,
\[
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{beginner} unindexedwhole\, invariantmass = \sum_{aggregate=1}^{beginner-1} wholesum( invariantmass - successormass ) + entiresum\, steadyfactor
\]

Now the series \( \sum_{aggregate=1}^{\infty} wholesum( invariantmass - successormass ) \) is absolutely convergent, for
\[
\lvert wholesum( invariantmass - successormass ) \rvert \le unbounded( invariantmass - successormass )
\]
and \( \sum_{aggregate=1}^{\infty} ( invariantmass - successormass ) \) converges to \( terminalmass \).
Moreover, \( \lim_{beginner \to \infty} entiresum\, steadyfactor = 0 \) because \( \{ entiresum \} \) is bounded. Therefore,
\[
\lim_{beginner \to \infty} \sum_{aggregate=1}^{beginner} unindexedwhole\, invariantmass = \sum_{aggregate=1}^{\infty} wholesum( invariantmass - successormass )
\]

Thus the theorem is proved.
For the particular problem, take \( steadyfactor = 1 / beginner \) and the result is immediate.

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
7. Show that if the series \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+mnbvcxqe+\cdots+plokijuy+\cdots \) converges, then the series \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla 2+mnbvcxqe^{\prime} 3+\cdots+plokijuy^{\prime} klmnopqr+\cdots \) converges also.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. This is a special case of a result sometimes called Abel's summation theorem: If the partial sums of the series \( \Sigma plokijuy \) are bounded and the sequence \( \left\{zxcvbnml\right\} \) decreases to zero, then \( \Sigma plokijuy zxcvbnml \) is convergent.

This theorem can be proved as follows. Let \( yhnujmik=\sum_{qplmokni=1}^{bvcxzasd} asdfghjk \) and let \( zswedcfg \) be a bound for \( \left\{\left|yhnujmik\right|\right\} \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp lkjhgfdp+hjgrksla poiuytre & +\cdots+plokijuy zxcvbnml \\
& =rfvtgbyh lkjhgfdp+\left(vtgbyhnu-rfvtgbyh\right) poiuytre+\cdots+\left(tfghbnji-ujmnhygt\right) zxcvbnml \\
& =rfvtgbyh\left(lkjhgfdp-poiuytre\right)+vtgbyhnu\left(poiuytre-qazwsxed\right)+\cdots+ujmnhygt\left(b_{n-1}-zxcvbnml\right)+tfghbnji zxcvbnml
\end{aligned}
\]
that is,
\[
\sum_{qplmokni=1}^{klmnopqr} asdfghjk qwertyui=\sum_{qplmokni=1}^{klmnopqr-1} ikmjnhuy\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right)+tfghbnji zxcvbnml
\]

Now the series \( \sum_{qplmokni=1}^{\infty} ikmjnhuy\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right) \) is absolutely convergent, for
\[
\left|ikmjnhuy\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right)\right| \leq zswedcfg\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right)
\]
and \( \sum_{qplmokni=1}^{\infty}\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right) \) converges to \( lkjhgfdp \).
Moreover, \( \lim _{klmnopqr \rightarrow \infty} tfghbnji zxcvbnml=0 \) because \( \left\{tfghbnji\right\} \) is bounded. Therefore,
\[
\lim _{klmnopqr \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{qplmokni=1}^{klmnopqr} asdfghjk qwertyui=\sum_{qplmokni=1}^{\infty} ikmjnhuy\left(qwertyui-edcrfvtg\right)
\]

Thus the theorem is proved.
For the particular problem, take \( zxcvbnml=1 / klmnopqr \) and the result is immediate.
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\bigl(a_{m,n}\bigr)_{m,n\ge 1}$ be a doubly indexed complex sequence and

\[
A_{M,N}:=\sum_{m=1}^{M}\sum_{n=1}^{N}a_{m,n}\qquad(M,N\in\mathbb{N})
\]

its rectangular partial sums.  
Assume that these sums are uniformly bounded, i.e.

\[
\lvert A_{M,N}\rvert\le K\qquad(M,N\ge 1) \tag{1}
\]

for some finite constant $K>0$.

Define the positive weight array  

\[
b_{m,n}:=\log\!\Bigl(1+\frac{1}{m}\Bigr)\,
          \log\!\Bigl(1+\frac{1}{n}\Bigr)\qquad(m,n\ge 1).          \tag{2}
\]

(a)  Prove that the limit  
\[
S:=\lim_{\,M,N\to\infty}\;
     \sum_{m=1}^{M}\sum_{n=1}^{N}a_{m,n}\,b_{m,n}                   \tag{3}
\]
exists, i.e. the double series with rectangular summation converges (possibly only conditionally).

(b)  Show the sharp bound  
\[
\lvert S\rvert\le K\,\bigl(\log 2\bigr)^{2}.                        \tag{4}
\]

(c)  Construct a sequence $\bigl(a_{m,n}\bigr)$ satisfying \textup{(1)} but for which  
\[
\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lvert a_{m,n}\rvert\,b_{m,n}=\infty,
\]
thereby proving that absolute convergence cannot be demanded in part (a).

(The first two items constitute a genuinely two-dimensional Abel-Dirichlet
theorem; the third item shows that estimate \textup{(4)} is best possible.)

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Dimensional Elevation.  The original exercise is one–dimensional;
    the variant demands a fully two–dimensional treatment.

2.  Advanced Tools.  Where the classical solution only needs Abel’s
    single-variable summation, the enhanced problem forces the competitor
    to perform a two–variable discrete integration by parts, keep track of
    mixed finite differences and boundary terms, and control them
    simultaneously.

3.  Non-trivial Weight Structure.  The new weights are products of
    logarithms, generating mixed differences whose behaviour must be
    analysed in both indices at once; simple monotonicity in one index
    is no longer sufficient.

4.  Uniform Bound Management.  One must recognise that boundedness of all
    rectangular partial sums is the correct analogue of “bounded partial
    sums” in one dimension and learn how to exploit it in a two-variable
    setting.

5.  Multiple Interacting Concepts.  The solution blends Dirichlet’s idea,
    multidimensional Abel summation, telescoping in two axes, positivity of
    mixed differences, and careful interchange of limits—significantly more
    conceptual load and technical detail than the original task.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $(a_{m,n})_{m,n\ge 1}$ be a doubly-indexed complex sequence and let  

  A_{M,N}\;=\;\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{M}\sum_{n=1}^{N}a_{m,n}\qquad (M,N\in\Bbb N)  

be its rectangular partial sums.  
Assume that these partial sums are uniformly bounded, i.e.  

  |A_{M,N}|\;\le K\qquad(M,N\ge 1)                                                    (1)

for some constant $K<\infty$.

Define the positive weight array  

  b_{m,n}\;=\;\log\!\Bigl(1+\frac1m\Bigr)\,\log\!\Bigl(1+\frac1n\Bigr),\qquad m,n\ge 1.     (2)

(a)  Prove that the limit  

  S\;=\;\lim_{M,N\to\infty}\;\sum_{m=1}^{M}\sum_{n=1}^{N} a_{m,n}\,b_{m,n}                     (3)

exists, i.e. the double series with rectangular summation converges (possibly only
conditionally).  

(b)  Show moreover that  

  |S|\;\le\;K\,(\log 2)^{2}.                                                               (4)

(c)  Give an example of a sequence $(a_{m,n})$ that satisfies hypothesis (1) but
for which  

  \displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|a_{m,n}|\,b_{m,n}=\infty,

thereby proving that absolute convergence cannot be demanded in (a).

(Parts (a)-(b) amount to a genuinely two-dimensional Abel-Dirichlet theorem.
Part (c) shows that the estimate (4) is the strongest possible.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Notation and discrete derivatives.  
For $f=(f_{m,n})_{m,n\ge 1}$ we use the forward difference operators  

  \Delta _{1}f_{m,n}=f_{m,n}-f_{m+1,n},  \Delta _{2}f_{m,n}=f_{m,n}-f_{m,n+1},  

and their composition  

  \Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}f_{m,n}=f_{m,n}-f_{m+1,n}-f_{m,n+1}+f_{m+1,n+1}\qquad(m,n\ge 1).     (5)

Extend $A_{M,N}$ to indices $0$ by $A_{0,N}=A_{M,0}=0$.  Then, for every $m,n\ge1$,  

  a_{m,n}=\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}A_{m-1,n-1}.                                                        (6)

(The single-step shift corrects the identity used in the original draft.)

2.  A two-dimensional Abel summation formula.  
Put  

  S_{M,N}:=\sum_{m=1}^{M}\sum_{n=1}^{N} a_{m,n}b_{m,n}.                                     (7)

With (6) and the change of indices $m\mapsto m+1,\;n\mapsto n+1$ we obtain  
\[
S_{M,N}= \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}A_{m,n}\bigr)\,b_{m+1,n+1}.
\]
Applying two successive discrete integrations by parts (i.e. moving the
operators $\Delta _{1},\Delta _{2}$ from $A$ to $b$) yields the exact identity

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
S_{M,N}
&=\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_{m,n}\,\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m+1,n+1} \\
&\quad+\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}A_{m,N}\,\Delta _{1}b_{m+1,N+1}
      +\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}A_{M,n}\,\Delta _{2}b_{M+1,n+1}
      +A_{M,N}\,b_{M+1,N+1}.
\end{aligned}}\qquad(8)
\]

(The calculation is a straightforward two-dimensional analogue of the
one-variable Abel transformation and is omitted for brevity.)

3.  Positivity and summability of the weight differences.  
Because $t\mapsto\log(1+1/t)$ is positive and strictly decreasing,  

  \Delta _{1}b_{m,n},\,\Delta _{2}b_{m,n},\,\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m,n}>0\qquad(m,n\ge1).     (9)

A direct factorisation gives

  \Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m,n}
     =\bigl(\log(1+\tfrac1m)-\log(1+\tfrac1{m+1})\bigr)
      \bigl(\log(1+\tfrac1n)-\log(1+\tfrac1{n+1})\bigr).             (10)

Therefore

  \sum _{m=1}^{\infty}\sum _{n=1}^{\infty}\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m,n}
     =\bigl(\log(1+\tfrac11)\bigr)^{2}=(\log 2)^{2}.             (11)

Furthermore, for every fixed $n$ one has  
  \sum _{m=1}^{\infty}\Delta _{1}b_{m,n}=b_{1,n}\le\log 2   and similarly  
  \sum _{n=1}^{\infty}\Delta _{2}b_{m,n}\le\log 2.                             (12)

4.  Passage to the limit.  
Using the bounds (1) and the positivity of the differences we can
estimate each term in (8) as follows:

(i)  Mixed term:

  | \sum _{m=0}^{M-1}\sum _{n=0}^{N-1} A_{m,n}\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m+1,n+1} |
     \leq K\sum _{m,n}\Delta _{1}\Delta _{2}b_{m,n}
     \leq K(\log 2)^{2}.                                           (13)

(ii)  Horizontal boundary:

  \bigl|\sum _{m=0}^{M-1}A_{m,N}\Delta _{1}b_{m+1,N+1}\bigr|
     \leq K\sum _{m=0}^{\infty}\Delta _{1}b_{m+1,N+1}
     =K\,b_{1,N+1}\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}0.                      (14)

(iii)  Vertical boundary is handled symmetrically and also tends to $0$.

(iv)  Corner term:

  |A_{M,N}|\;b_{M+1,N+1}\le K\,b_{M+1,N+1}\xrightarrow[M,N\to\infty]{}0.     (15)

Putting (13)-(15) together shows that the net
$\bigl(S_{M,N}\bigr)_{M,N\ge1}$ is Cauchy; hence the limit (3) exists and

  |S|\le K(\log 2)^{2},                                             (16)

which proves both statements (a) and (b).

5.  Why absolute convergence cannot be required (part (c)).  
Consider  

  a_{m,n}=(-1)^{m+n}\qquad(m,n\ge1).

Its rectangular sums oscillate between $-1,0,1$, so (1) holds with $K=1$.
However  

  |a_{m,n}|\,b_{m,n}=b_{m,n},

and  

  \sum _{m=1}^{\infty}b_{m,1}=\sum _{m=1}^{\infty}\log\!\bigl(1+\tfrac1m\bigr)=\infty,

hence  

  \sum _{m,n}|a_{m,n}|\,b_{m,n}=\infty.

The main series itself does converge, though, by (a)-(b); indeed
the estimate (16) gives $|S|\le(\log 2)^{2}$.

Thus the absolute version of part (a) is false, completing the answer to (c).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Dimensional Elevation.  The original exercise is one–dimensional;
    the variant demands a fully two–dimensional treatment.

2.  Advanced Tools.  Where the classical solution only needs Abel’s
    single-variable summation, the enhanced problem forces the competitor
    to perform a two–variable discrete integration by parts, keep track of
    mixed finite differences and boundary terms, and control them
    simultaneously.

3.  Non-trivial Weight Structure.  The new weights are products of
    logarithms, generating mixed differences whose behaviour must be
    analysed in both indices at once; simple monotonicity in one index
    is no longer sufficient.

4.  Uniform Bound Management.  One must recognise that boundedness of all
    rectangular partial sums is the correct analogue of “bounded partial
    sums” in one dimension and learn how to exploit it in a two-variable
    setting.

5.  Multiple Interacting Concepts.  The solution blends Dirichlet’s idea,
    multidimensional Abel summation, telescoping in two axes, positivity of
    mixed differences, and careful interchange of limits—significantly more
    conceptual load and technical detail than the original task.
